Symptoms of Mycoplasma Pneumonia are often mild and appear over 1 to 3 weeks. They may become more severe in some people. Common symptoms include any of the following: chest pain, chills, cough (usually dry and not bloody), excessive sweating, fever (may be high), headache, and sore throat. Lesson common symptoms include ear pain, soreness, muscle aches and joint stiffness, neck lump, rapid breathing, and rash.

Symptoms for Haemophilus Influenzae usually come on quickly, and may include fever and chills, mental status and changes, nausea and vomiting, sensitivity to light, severe headache, and stiff neck. Other symptoms that can onccur include agitation, bulging fontanelles in infants, decreased consciousness, poor feeding and irritability in children, rapid breathing, and unsual posture with the head and neck arched backwards.

Symptoms of Streptococcus infection depend on the type and may include a sore, red throat with swollen tonsils and white spots (strep throat), a red rash on the body (scarlet fever), skin infection (impetigo), and in more severe cases blood infections, pneumonia, meningitis, and urinary tract infections.

Symptoms of Klebsiella (Respiratory) include sores in the anal area, small beefy-red bumps on the genitals or around the anus that may develop into raised, velvety red nodules (granulation tissue), lesions that bleed easily but are often painless, slow destruction of genital tissue, possible spread to the groin, and loss of skin color around the affected areas.

Symptoms of acute bronchitis include chest discomfort, a cough that produces mucus (which may be clear or yellow-green), fatigue, low-grade fever, shortness of breath that worsens with activity, and wheezing in people with asthma. After the infection clears, a dry, nagging cough may persist for 1 to 4 weeks.

Symptoms of Bronchiectasis include a long-term (chronic) cough with large amounts of foul-smelling sputum, along with breath odor, coughing up blood (less common in children), fatigue, paleness, shortness of breath that worsens with exercise, weight loss, wheezing, low-grade fever, night sweats, and, in rare cases, clubbing of the fingers depending on the underlying cause.
Symptoms of Pharyngitis is sore throat. Other symptoms may include fever, headache, joint pain and muscle aches, skin rashes, and swollen lymph nodes (glands) in the neck.

Symptoms of Psittacosis include a 5 to 15 day incubation period followed by symptoms such as blood-tinged sputum, dry cough, fatigue, fever and chills, headache, joint aches, muscle aches (most often in the head and neck), shortness of breath, diarrhea, swelling in the back of the throat (pharyngitis), inflammation of the liver, and confusion.

Symptoms of urethritis in men include blood in the urine or semen, burning pain while urinating (dysuria), discharge from the penis, fever (rare), frequent or urgent urination, itching, tenderness or swelling in the penis, enlarged lymph nodes in the groin area, and pain during intercourse or ejaculation. In women, symptoms may include abdominal pain, burning pain while urinating, fever and chills, frequent or urgent urination, pelvic pain, pain during intercourse, and vaginal discharge.

Symptoms of Cystitis include cloudy or bloody urine, strong or foul-smelling urine, low fever (most people will not have a fever), pain or burning with urination, pressure or cramping in the lower middle abdomen or back, and strong need to urinate often, even right after the bladder has been emptied.

Symptoms of Pyelonephritis include cloudy or bloody urine which may have a fould or strong odor, low grade fever in some people, pain or burning with urination, pressure or cramping in the lower abdomen or back, and strong need to urinate often even right after the bladd has been emptied. If the infection spread to your kidneys, symptoms may include chills and shaking, fatigue and generally ill feeling, fever above 38.3°C, pain (side, back, or groin), flushed, warmed, or reddened skin, confusion, nausea and vomiting, and sometime severe abdominal pain.

Symptoms of interstitial nephritis may range from mild to severe kidney problems, including acute kidney failure, and in about half of cases may involve decreased urine output and other signs of kidney failure; these symptoms include blood in the urine, fever, increased or decreased urine output, mental status changes (such as drowsiness, confusion, or coma), nausea and vomiting, rash, swelling in any area of the body, and weight gain due to fluid retention.

Symptoms of prostatitis can start suddenly with chills, fever, flushing of the skin, lower abdominal tenderness, and body aches, while chronic prostatitis presents with similar but milder symptoms that develop more slowly and may have symptom-free periods between episodes; urinary symptoms include blood in the urine, burning or pain during urination, difficulty starting urination or emptying the bladder, foul-smelling urine, and a weak urine stream, while other symptoms may include pain or aching in the lower abdomen above the pubic bone, lower back, perineum, or testicles, pain with ejaculation or blood in the semen, and pain during bowel movements, and in some cases may be associated with infection in or around the testicles (epididymitis).
Symptoms of sinusitis can include fever, weakness, fatigue, cough, and congestion. There may also be mucus drainage in the back of the throat, called postnasal drip.

Symptoms of Legionella pneumophila infection (Legionnaires’ disease) usually appear 2 to 14 days after exposure and include cough, high fever, chills, headache, chest pain, shortness of breath, fatigue, nausea and vomiting, diarrhea, and confusion.

Symptoms of Bordetella pertussis infection (whooping cough) usually occur in three stages. In the first stage, symptoms are similar to a common cold and may include a runny nose, mild fever, mild cough, and in infants, pauses in breathing (apnea). In the second stage, symptoms become more severe and include intense coughing fits followed by a high-pitched “whoop” sound, vomiting after coughing, and extreme fatigue after coughing episodes. In the third stage, the symptoms begin to improve, with a milder and less frequent cough during recovery.

Symptoms of Mycoplasma Pneumonia are often mild and appear over 1 to 3 weeks. They may become more severe in some people. Lesson common symptoms include ear pain, soreness, muscle aches and joint stiffness, neck lump, rapid breathing, and rash.

Symptoms of Chlamydia trachomatis infection often do not appear, so many people may not realize they are infected. When symptoms do occur, they may appear several weeks after exposure. In women, symptoms can include abnormal vaginal discharge that may have a strong smell, a burning sensation when urinating, and if the infection spreads, lower abdominal pain, pain during sex, nausea, and fever. In men, symptoms may include discharge from the penis, a burning sensation when urinating, and pain or swelling in one or both testicles (less common). If the infection affects the rectum, it can cause rectal pain, discharge, and bleeding in both men and women.
Symptoms of Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) infection are not specifically listed in the text, but it is described as a type of lung infection that often affects people with HIV.

Symptoms of Mycobacterium leprae infection include skin lesions or damaged skin areas, loss of sensation in affected areas, nerve damage causing numbness (especially in the hands and feet), muscle weakness, and in severe cases vision problems, nasal tissue damage, and shortening or loss of fingers or toes, with some patients also experiencing fever and painful nodules during reaction episodes.

Symptoms of Campylobacter infection typically start 2 to 4 days after exposure and may last about a week, and include cramping abdominal pain, fever, nausea and vomiting, and watery diarrhea that may sometimes be bloody.

Symptoms of gonorrhea may not always be present; in men, it can cause pain when urinating and discharge from the penis, while in women, early symptoms are often mild but may include bleeding between periods, pain when urinating, and increased vaginal discharge.

Symptoms of Streptococcus infection depend on the type and may include a sore, red throat with swollen tonsils and white spots (strep throat), a red rash on the body (scarlet fever), skin infection (impetigo), and in more severe cases blood infections, pneumonia, meningitis, and urinary tract infections.

Symptoms of Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) include a red, swollen, and painful area on the skin that may drain pus or other fluids and look like a boil, especially where the skin has been cut or rubbed, and in more severe infections (often in health care settings) symptoms may include chest pain, cough or shortness of breath, fatigue, fever and chills, general ill feeling, headache, rash, and wounds that do not heal.

Symptoms of soft tissue infection may include a small, red, painful lump or bump on the skin that spreads, a rapidly enlarging bruise-like area that may develop within an hour, a center that becomes dark, dusky, and then black as the tissue dies, and skin that may break open and ooze fluid. Other symptoms may include feeling ill, fever, sweating, chills, nausea, dizziness, weakness, and shock.

Symptoms of duodenal ulcer include a burning stomach pain that often starts between meals or during the night, may briefly improve after eating or taking antacids, lasts for minutes to hours, and can come and go for several days or weeks.

Symptoms of atypical pneumonia include chills, cough (which may produce bloody mucus in Legionella pneumonia), fever that may be mild or high, and shortness of breath that may occur mainly with exertion. Other symptoms include chest pain that worsens with deep breathing or coughing, confusion (especially in older adults or those with Legionella pneumonia), headache, loss of appetite, low energy and fatigue, muscle aches and joint stiffness, sweating and clammy skin, and in some cases diarrhea and vomiting, ear pain, eye pain or soreness, neck lump, rash, and sore throat (more commonly seen with Mycoplasma pneumonia).

Symptoms of acute bronchitis include chest discomfort, a cough that produces mucus (which may be clear or yellow-green), fatigue, low-grade fever, shortness of breath that worsens with activity, and wheezing in people with asthma. After the infection clears, a dry, nagging cough may persist for 1 to 4 weeks.

Symptoms of chronic bronchitis may be mild or absent at first but typically worsen over time, and can include frequent coughing or a cough that produces a lot of mucus, wheezing, a whistling or squeaky sound when breathing, shortness of breath especially during physical activity, and a feeling of tightness in the chest; some individuals may also experience frequent respiratory infections such as colds and the flu, and in severe cases, weight loss, weakness in the lower muscles, and swelling in the ankles, feet, or legs.

Symptoms for Haemophilus Influenzae usually come on quickly, and may include fever and chills, mental status and changes, nausea and vomiting, sensitivity to light, severe headache, and stiff neck. Other symptoms that can onccur include agitation, bulging fontanelles in infants, decreased consciousness, poor feeding and irritability in children, rapid breathing, and unsual posture with the head and neck arched backwards.

Symptoms of Mycoplasma Pneumonia are often mild and appear over 1 to 3 weeks. They may become more severe in some people. Common symptoms include any of the following: chest pain, chills, cough (usually dry and not bloody), excessive sweating, fever (may be high), headache, and sore throat. Lesson common symptoms include ear pain, soreness, muscle aches and joint stiffness, neck lump, rapid breathing, and rash.

Symptoms of Streptococcus infection depend on the type and may include a sore, red throat with swollen tonsils and white spots (strep throat), a red rash on the body (scarlet fever), skin infection (impetigo), and in more severe cases blood infections, pneumonia, meningitis, and urinary tract infections.

Symptoms of Cystitis include cloudy or bloody urine, strong or foul-smelling urine, low fever (most people will not have a fever), pain or burning with urination, pressure or cramping in the lower middle abdomen or back, and strong need to urinate often, even right after the bladder has been emptied.

Symptoms of urethritis in men include blood in the urine or semen, burning pain while urinating (dysuria), discharge from the penis, fever (rare), frequent or urgent urination, itching, tenderness or swelling in the penis, enlarged lymph nodes in the groin area, and pain during intercourse or ejaculation. In women, symptoms may include abdominal pain, burning pain while urinating, fever and chills, frequent or urgent urination, pelvic pain, pain during intercourse, and vaginal discharge.

Symptoms of asymptomatic bacteriuria include no symptoms at all; however, burning during urination, increased urgency to urinate, and increased frequency of urination are not present in this condition and instead suggest a urinary tract infection.

Symptoms of urinary tract infection include pain or burning when urinating, fever, tiredness or shakiness, an urge to urinate often, pressure in the lower abdomen, urine that smells bad or looks cloudy or reddish, and pain in the back or side below the ribs.

Symptoms of lower respiratory tract infection (such as RSV infection) include runny nose, decreased appetite, cough, sneezing, fever, and wheezing.
Symptoms of Pharyngitis is sore throat. Other symptoms may include fever, headache, joint pain and muscle aches, skin rashes, and swollen lymph nodes (glands) in the neck.

Symptoms of acute otitis media vary by age. In infants, the main signs may include irritability or inconsolable crying, fever, and trouble sleeping, while in older children and adults symptoms can include ear pain, fullness in the ear, general feeling of illness, nasal congestion, cough, lethargy, vomiting, diarrhea, hearing loss in the affected ear, drainage of fluid from the ear, and loss of appetite.
Symptoms of sinusitis can include fever, weakness, fatigue, cough, and congestion. There may also be mucus drainage in the back of the throat, called postnasal drip.

Symptoms of Lyme disease include a slowly expanding red rash (often bull’s-eye shaped) at the site of the tick bite, flu-like symptoms, enlarged lymph nodes, additional skin rashes, fatigue, headache, muscle and joint aches, facial paralysis, numbness or weakness in the hands or feet, difficulty concentrating or memory problems, heart palpitations or irregular heartbeat, and joint pain and swelling, especially in the knees.

Symptoms of Legionella pneumophila infection (Legionnaires’ disease) usually appear 2 to 14 days after exposure and include cough, high fever, chills, headache, chest pain, shortness of breath, fatigue, nausea and vomiting, diarrhea, and confusion.

Symptoms of tuberculosis (TB) include a persistent cough, sometimes lasting weeks, chest pain, shortness of breath, fatigue, and fever. In more severe cases, TB can also cause chills, night sweats, weight loss, and coughing up blood.

Symptoms of Bronchiectasis include a long-term (chronic) cough with large amounts of foul-smelling sputum, along with breath odor, coughing up blood (less common in children), fatigue, paleness, shortness of breath that worsens with exercise, weight loss, wheezing, low-grade fever, night sweats, and, in rare cases, clubbing of the fingers depending on the underlying cause.
